coredata.dc:title: Probing heteroatoms co-doped graphene quantum dots for energy transfer and 2-photon assisted applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sagar, Vijay Kumar
Dey, Soumyodeep
Bhattacharya, Shubhayan
Lesani, Pooria
Ramaswamy, Yogambha
Singh, Gurvinder
Zreiqat, Hala
Bisht, Prem Ballabh
coredata.dc:description: Carbon based quantum dots find various applications such as imaging, diagnosis and therapeutics due to low toxicity, antibacterial activity, excellent water solubility, and good biocompatibility. However, the UV-blue emission of carbon based quantum dots is unsuited for applications in biomedicine and imaging as the short wavelength lights harm the living cells due to phototoxicity. In the present work, we have synthesised the long wavelength emitting heteroatoms (nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S)) co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQD) via hydrothermal route. These NS-GQD have excitation-wavelength independent photoluminescence with high quantum yield in protonated and neutral forms. The non-covalent interaction of these NS-GQD when studied with a dye rhodamine B (RB) shows evidence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). It has been shown here that the NS-GQD-RB composite exhibits prominent two-photon absorption at near infrared wavelength with a capability of deep-tissue imaging upto 175 µm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot
Rhodamine B
Förster resonance energy transfer
2-photon absorption
Deep-tissue imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of heteroatoms co-doped graphene quantum dots
Structural and optical characterization of NS-GQD
FRET capability of NS-GQD with organic dye
Two-photon absorption process and deep disuse imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded under IIT Madras grants (i). TR20210297PHIITM000081 & (ii). PH1920253ALUM000081. This work is also part of the activities of “Centre of Excellence in Biosensing” funded by the Ministry of Education, New Delhi vide project number SB20210819PHMHRD000081. One of us (VKS) thanks University Grant Commission, New Delhi for a fellowship and SD thanks Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi for Inspire fellowship.

This work is funded under IIT Madras grants (i). TR20210297PHIITM000081 & (ii). PH1920253ALUM000081. This work is also part of the activities of “Centre of Excellence in Biosensing” funded by the Ministry of Education, New Delhi vide project number SB20210819PHMHRD000081. One of us (VKS) thanks University Grant Commission, New Delhi for a fellowship and SD thanks Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi for Inspire fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the bottom-up synthesis of NS-GQD.

A) FTIR spectrum of NS-GQD (the inset in panel A shows the vibrational modes of heteroatoms (S, N) attached to the GQD). B) Raman spectrum of NS-GQD with the Lorentzian fit of D and G bands of NS-GQD, respectively. C) TEM image of NS-GQD (inset is the histogram showing size distribution of NS-GQD). D) HRTEM image of NS-GQD. E) AFM height image of disk-shaped NS-GQD with the average thickness of 2.25 nm.

pH-dependent absorption (panel A) and PL spectra (panel B) of NS-GQD. The inset of panel B gives the as prepared NS-GQD under daylight exposure (a: pale yellow) and excitation at 410 nm (b: green emission). PL spectra of NS-GQD as a function of excitation wavelength at pH 7 (panel C). The deconvolution of one of the PL spectrum (panel D).

Panel A: Absorption spectra of NS-GQD with varying concentrations of RB. The inset of panel A gives a magnified portion to indicate the direct excitation process. Panel B: PL intensity of NS-GQD (43 µg/mL) in the presence of [RB] as indicated (λex. = 430 nm). The inset of panel B gives linear fit to the ratio of the PL intensities (open circle) at the emission wavelengths of the acceptor (
F 578
) to that of donor (
F 502
) as a function of [RB]. Panel C: Spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of the RB (14 μM) and the PL spectrum of the NS-GQD (200 μg/ml) (
λ
ex .
=
430 nm). Schematic of the FRET between NS-GQD and RB (panel D).

FRET efficiency as a function of distance (R) between the donor (D) and acceptor (A). Here
R 0
is the Förster distance.

Panel A: PL decay profile for NS-GQD (43
μ g / m l
) in presence of RB (28
μ
M) monitored at 500 nm. Panel B shows that the decay profile monitored at 600 nm. The symbols are the data points and the solid curves are the best fit with 2 components (panel A) and a single component (panel B) of eq. (8). IRF is the instrument response function. The panels C and D show the corresponding residuals for the corresponding fits, respectively. Panel E: The reduction of the donor decay constants (I) at 500 nm and the rise of the acceptor decay constants (II) at 600 nm. Inset gives the linear fit with equation (9). The excitation, and relaxation processes of the donor and acceptor pair are shown in panel F.
k
nr
,
k r
represents the non-radiative and radiative decay rates of the donor.
k
r
'
represents the radiative decay rate of the acceptor. The FRET rate from donor to acceptor is represented by
k
FRET
.
Panel A: Absorption and PL spectra of N-RCDs at pH 3. The inset of panel A gives the as prepared N-RCD under excitation at 532 nm (red emission). Panel B: Proliferation of.

The schematic of 2PA process inside the pigskin tissue of thickness of 2000
μ m
. The energy level diagram indicates the simultaneous absorption of 2 photons of near infrared light source (780 nm).

head.ce:title: Probing heteroatoms co-doped graphene quantum dots for energy transfer and 2-photon assisted applications

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai 600036, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon based quantum dots find various applications such as imaging, diagnosis and therapeutics due to low toxicity, antibacterial activity, excellent water solubility, and good biocompatibility. However, the UV-blue emission of carbon based quantum dots is unsuited for applications in biomedicine and imaging as the short wavelength lights harm the living cells due to phototoxicity. In the present work, we have synthesised the long wavelength emitting heteroatoms (nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S)) co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQD) via hydrothermal route. These NS-GQD have excitation-wavelength independent photoluminescence with high quantum yield in protonated and neutral forms. The non-covalent interaction of these NS-GQD when studied with a dye rhodamine B (RB) shows evidence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). It has been shown here that the NS-GQD-RB composite exhibits prominent two-photon absorption at near infrared wavelength with a capability of deep-tissue imaging upto 175 µm.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are economical and have wide applications in optoelectronic devices [1], bioimaging [2–5], due to their low toxicity [4,6], tunable photoluminescence (PL) [7] and nonlinear optical properties [8,9]. GQDs possess sp2- hybridized carbon atoms with sizes less than 100 nm [10,11]. The quasi-zero dimenional GQD is a wide bandgap semiconductor [1,12] in contrast to the graphene with a negligible bandgap [13]. The bandgap of GQD can be tuned by changing its lateral dimension as well as by doping [14] with metal atoms [15] and non-metal heteroatoms [16]. Heteroatom doping relies on the electron-donating or electron-withdrawing capabilities of the dopant. It has been observed that co-doping with heteroatoms not only improves the PL quantum yield of the systems but also provides more active sites [17]. This brodens the application of GQD to biosensing [18,19], photocatalysis [14], and energy transfer processes [3]. In addition, GQD has also been used for photodynamic therapy [4,5] due to their low toxicity and photostability. Reduction in the toxicity levels are proposed largely through the introduction of the dopants such as nitrogen (N) [20], boron (B) [21], phosphorus (P) [22], and sulfur (S) [23] or a combination of different dopants, a process known as co-doping [18]. In addition to the doping, without altering their periodic structure the non-covalent functionzaltion of the graphene derivatives with an organic dye has been reported earlier [24]. The charge transfer interaction between a fluorescent dye and GQD leads to the enhancement in the radiative intensity of the dye [25] as compared to graphene which quenches the radiative intensity [26].

It is observed that the PL spectra of GQD overlap with absorption spectra of various organic dyes, for instance rhodamine B (RB). The absorption spectrum of the GQD (donor) and fluorescence spectrum of the dye (acceptor) exhibits an overlap indicating the feasibility of resonance energy transfer process. The FÖrster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer process from donor to acceptor via long-range dipole–dipole interaction. The interaction between donor and acceptor can leads to the fluorescence quenching of the donor by either electron transfer (ET) or FRET. Upon photoexcitation, an electron is transferred from the donor to the acceptor leading to the creation of charge separated bi-radicals. The photoinduced electron transfer depends on the energy levels of the interacting systems, polarity of the solvent, and distance between them [27]. Both the ET and FRET processes depend on the distance between the donor and acceptor systems but the rate of ET is much faster than the rate of FRET because of its distance dependent exponential nature [28]. At large distances, the ET rate is expected to become much smaller than the rate of FRET. The FRET technique has widely used for fluorescence imaging, maping nanoscale distances and fabricating biosensing devices [29–31]. Moreover, the multiphoton processes find applications to probe an organism’s tissue at various depths to acquire knowledge about the structure and various biological activities taking place deep inside the organism [32].

In this work, we have adopted an economical and sophisticated bottom-up approach based on hydrothermal route to synthesize highly fluorescent and biocompatible nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQD). The structural and optical properties of NS-GQD have been investigated in the detail. The FRET probe is constructed by the fucntionalization of RB dye with the NS-GQD because of their spectral overlap. The phenomenon of FRET between NS-GQD to RB has been studied in detail. The potential of NS-GQD for deep tissue imaging has been explored with multiphoton excitation at 780 nm. This helps to check the capabilities of NS-GQD for imaging of the thick, highly scattering specimens.

Naphthalene, o-phenylenediamine, and thiourea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Rhodamine B was purchased from Serva international was used as received. All other chemicals viz, methanol, acetone, deionized (DI) water, HCl, and KOH were of analytic grade and were used as received.

Due to reduced photodamage, the PL in the longer wavelength region is quite favorable for the range of biomedical applications including ultrasensitive biosensing, deep tissue imaging, diagnosis and therapeutics. Consequently, an economical and sophisticated one step hydrothermal route has been used to synthesize heteroatoms doped carbon based quantum dots. To compare the usefulness of these quantum dots for biological applications different precursor were used adjusting the pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of heteroatoms co-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Naphthalene, a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon was used as a precursor, and thiourea was used as a doping compound to synthesize heteroatoms co-doped graphene quantum dots using a hydrothermal procedure. The schematic of the nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped GQD (NS-GQD) synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

Density functional theory (DFT) estimates show that the absence of bonding energy in naphthalene does not allow polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons to fuse in to GQD [33]. To fabricate the heteroatoms co-doped GQD, the nitration of naphthalene is necessary. The stirring of naphthalene (1 gm) in 80 mL of concentrated nitric acid at 80 °C for 12 hr leads to the nitration of the naphthalene. The nitrated naphthalene contains a mixture of two dinitro compounds such as 1,5- dinitro and 1,8-dinitro and a small residue of 1-nitronapthalene [33,34]. 1-nitronaphthalene does not contribute in the process of GQD because the benzene ring without a nitro group cannot be activated for dehydrogenation.

The heteroatoms co-doped GQD was synthesized by mixing 20 mg of dinitronapthalene and 20 mg of thiourea in DI water (10 mL). The mixture was stirred for 30 min and transferred to a teflon lined autoclave and treated at 180 °C for 8 hr. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min and the sediment was removed. The as-prepared GQD were dialyzed for 2 days. The final product was collected after freeze-drying for a day and named as NS-GQD.

The absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–visible dual-beam spectrometer (Jasco, V570). The PL spectra were measured with a spectrofluorometer (Jasco, FP-6600) in the front face geometry. Raman spectrum was recorded with a Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon, model HR-800) equipped with a He-Ne laser (632.8 nm). The spectral resolution of the Raman system was 2 cm−1. The size determination of NS-GQD was done using transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded to determine the functional groups attached to NS-GQD with the FTIR spectrometer (Jasco, 4100). The atomic force microscope (AFM) was used to measure the topographic height of NS-GQD with Park Systems (model NX10). Time-resolved measurements were carried out by using the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The excitation was done with a 408 nm ps diode laser (1 MHz, Advanced Photonics). A commercial software FAST (Edinburgh Instruments) was used to analyze the data by deconvolution method. The goodness of the fit was judged by
χ
2
and residuals. For a good fit, the
χ
2
was close to 1 and the residuals were evenly distributed around the zero line.

The nonlinear optical measurements were carried out by the open aperture (OA) Z-scan technique with the second harmonic of a Nd: YAG laser (Quanta system). The laser operates at the repetition rate of 10 Hz and has a pulse duration of 100 ps as described elsewhere [26]. The sample was scanned in the focal plane of a lens of a focal length of 5 cm by pumping with 532 nm. This setup gives an intensity of 12.44
G W /
c m
2
and the beam waist of 17
μ m
at the focal point. The Rayleigh range in this configuration is 1.7 mm. The transmitted light was detected by a photodiode (PDI 400, Becker and Hickl). The data was recorded with the LabVIEW software on a PC.

Human breast cancer (MCF-7) cell line was obtained from the American Type Culture Collection and the cells were cultured in complete cell culture medium comprising of DMEM (Gibco laboratories, USA) supplemented with, 10% fetal calf serum (Life technologies, USA) and 30 mg/mL penicillin, and 100 mg/mL streptomycin (Life Technologies, USA). Cells were cultured at 37 °C with 5% CO2, and the medium was refreshed every 3 days until 80–90% confluency was achieved.

MTS assay was used to compare the effect of quantum dots on cell viability. MCF-7 cells were seeded in a 48-well plate at a density of 20,000 cells/well and incubated for 24 hr at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in complete medium. After 24 hr the growth medium was then replaced with DMEM containing quantum dots at a concentration of 50 µg/mL and incubated for 1, 3 and 7 days. Cells cultured on tissue culture plastic was used as control. MCF-7 viability was assessed using CellTiter 96 AQueous One Solution Reagent (Promega), which contains a tetrazolium compound [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium, inner salt; MTS] that is reduced by cells to yield a water-soluble formazan dye. At the end of each time point, 200 μL of MTS solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 hr at 37 °C under 5% CO2, and then 100 μL of the reacted reagent from each well was transferred to a 96-well plate for the absorbance measurement at a wavelength of 490 nm (Thermo Scientific Multiskan).

The pigskin of thickness 2000 μm was separated with a Leica VT 1200S Vibratome. Subsequently, 100 μg/mL of NS-GQD was incubated into the portion of pigskin tissue for 3 hr and then washed with PBS buffer before the measurements. The NS-GQD incubated pigskin were placed on the glass substrate and covered with cover slip under the microscope. The tissue imaging was done with a 2-photon (2P) confocal microscope equipped with a multiphoton laser (Leica TCS SP5 II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of NS-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectrum of the synthesized NS-GQD is shown in Fig. 2 A. The good hydrophilic property of NS-GQD is indicated by the strong peak due to the stretching of OH at 3290 cm−1. The presence of vibrational bands due to the bending of C–C at 2117 cm−1 and C = C at 1631 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum suggest the graphitic structure of NS-GQD. The stretching vibration of S = O at 1045 cm−1, C-N at 1140 cm−1, and C-NH-C peak centered at 1388 cm−1 indicate the doping of S and N in the graphitic lattice, respectively [33,35].

The Raman spectrum shown in Fig. 2B displays the two characteristic bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 corresponding to the D-band (disorder) and G-band (graphitic), respectively. The D-band is indicative of the breathing mode of the sp2 ring that is related to the defects as a result of the heteroatom co-doping in the GQD lattice [18,26]. On the other hand, G-band is related to the in-plane bond stretching of sp2 carbon atoms [26]. The
I D
/
I G
ratio of 1.15 suggests the disorder in the sp2 carbon lattice. Fig. 2C and D showed the TEM and HRTEM images of NS-GQD, suggesting the formation of crystalline NS-GQD in the size range of 20–30 nm with lattice parameter 0.22 nm corresponding to (100) facet of graphene. The results from AFM height image reveal the disk-shaped morphology of NS-GQD with average thickness of 2.25 nm, thus indicating the multilayered structure in NS-GQD (Fig. 1E).

We measured the absorption and PL spectra of NS-GQD at three different pH values (Fig. 3 A and B). The absorption spectra of NS-GQD displayed a strong peak in the UV region, two bands followed by a weak shoulder peak at 530 nm in the visible (Fig. 3A). As the pH of the environment increased from 2 to 12, the absorption peak centered at 288 nm is red-shifted to 314 nm, whereas the absorption bands at 430, 458 nm gradually become prominent. The absorption band at 550 nm is prominent in acidic environement and becomes less prominent with the blue-shift (530 nm) in the neutral environement and finally disappeared in alkaline environement. The variations in the pH leads to the process of protonation and deprotonation which consequently affects the distribution of π electron cloud density between the carbon backbone and surface states and resulting in the shift in the absorption band positions [36]. The appearance of these absorption bands can be assigned to the electron transitions in the aromatic sp2 domains and presence of heteroatoms in the NS-GQD. The absorption peak at 288 nm is related to the π → π* transition of C = N and the absorption at 310 nm corresponds to n → π* transition of the C = O bond [37,38]. The absorption band at 430 nm can be assigned due to the n → π* transition of the conjugated C = O and C = N bonds [39]. The n → π* transition of the conjugated C = N and C = S bonds gives rise to the absorption band at 460 nm [40]. The absorption band at 530 nm is attributed to the n → π* transition of the conjugated C = S bond [40]. On excitation at 430 nm, the PL spectrum of the NS-GQD shows higher PL intensity at pH 7 in contrast to that of pH 2 and 12 (Fig. 3B). Also, the PL quantum yield (QY) of as-prepared NS-GQD is higher in the neutral environment than the acidic (protonated) or basic (deprotonated) environments. The fluorescence quantum yields of NS-GQD at pH 2, 7, 12 are 11 %, 20 %, and 12 %, respectively. The fluorescence decay profiles of NS-GQD as a function of pH were also recorded. The observed profiles remain biexponential with the fluorescence decay times higher in neutral environment as compared to protonated and de-pronated environment. The GQD are negatively charged due to delocalized π-electrons and also often contain other negatively charged functional groups. The pH response towards the PL of GQD can be assigned to the aggregation and disaggregation induced by the deprotonation and protonation of the functional group on the surface of GQD [41]. Addition of NaOH leads to the deprotonation of the attached functional groups resulting in the dissociation of hydrogen bonds. As the concentration of OH− increased, the crystal nuclei begin to grow and resulting in the decrease in the concentration of NS-GQD. The process of aggregation leads to the PL quenching. The edge-states can also be assigned to the origin of PL in GQD [42]. When the pH value of the environment decreases, the zig-zag sites of GQD undergo a protonation process and the structure of the triplet carbene is destroyed. This disruption changes the edge states resulting in the PL intensity decrease. The synthesized NS-GQD appear yellow under the daylight exposure and look green under UV light irradiation (405 nm) (see inset in Fig. 3B). PL spectra of NS-GQD (at pH 7) as a function of excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 3C. It can be noticed that on increasing the excitation wavelength, the corresponding PL intensity increases but the peak position does not change. This reveals the excitation-wavelength independent PL behaviour of NS-GQD. To understand the origin of the PL, the spectrum at pH 7 was deconvoluted using Gaussian functions (Fig. 3D).

Peak I is related to the intrinsic nature due to the quantum confinement. On the other hand, peak II is attributed to the surface and defect states. The excitation independent PL mechanism can be attributed to the uniform particle size distribution of carbon skeleton [43] and surface molecular state [44]. Reducing the oxygen atoms (non-radiative paths) and incorporation of heteroatoms (S, N) unfold the new radiative paths which enhances the PL quantum yield (20 %) as well [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET capability of NS-GQD with organic dye

ce:section.ce:para: A series of absorption spectra with varying concentrations of RB in the absence and presence of NS-GQD were recorded to estimate the strength of ground state interaction between NS-GQD and RB. Fig. 4 A displays the absorption spectra at a fixed concentration of NS-GQD in presence of different concentration of RB.

The absorption maximum of RB appears at 553 nm with a shoulder at 510 nm. On addition of RB, we did not observe any changes in the absorption spectra. This indicates no interaction in the ground state between NS-GQD and RB dye.

The PL spectra of the donor as a function of the acceptor concentration were collected (Fig. 4B). Upon excitation at 430 nm (lowest absorption region for the acceptor), NS-GQD shows a single PL band at 500 nm. On increasing the concentration of RB, the PL intensity of the NS-GQD decreases with the concomitant increase in the PL intensity of RB. Apart from the feasibility of non-radiative energy transfer, the PL enhancement of RB caused by direct excitation at 430 nm, and self-absorption cannot be ruled out. The inset in Fig. 4B shows a linear trend in the ratio of the PL intensities of the acceptor to the donor as a function of acceptor concentration. By combining the spectroscopic properties of the RB and NS-GQD, the interaction between the two can be explained based on the electrostatic interaction of the positively charged aminoxanthene group of RB interacting with the negatively charged NS-GQD and geometry specific π-π interaction between the two. The spectral overlap between the PL of the donor and absorbance of the acceptor can be seen in the Fig. 4C. The distance-dependent feasibility of FRET mechanism is schematically illustrated in the Fig. 4D.

The spectral overlap integral
J
λ
between PL of NS-GQD and absorption of RB was calculated from eq. (1) [45] to estimate the Förster radius (R 0). The Förster radius (R 0) corresponds to the distance between the donor and the acceptor at which the probability of energy transfer is 50 %.
(1)
J
λ
=
∫ ∞
0
f D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ

where ε (λ) is the molar extinction coefficient of the acceptor at wavelength λ and
f D
( λ
) is the corrected PL emission intensity of the donor in the spectral range of λ to (λ + dλ). R 0 can be obtained as follows from the value of J (λ) [45].
(2)
R 0
= 9.78 × 103
k 2 . n - 4 . QD .
J
λ
1 / 6

Here, k2 is the donor/acceptor transition dipole orientation taken to be 2/3. QD is the donor (NS-GQD) PL quantum yield,
J
(λ) is the spectral overlap, and n is the refractive index of the water taken to be 1.33. From the spectral overlap
( J
λ
= 1.82 ×
10
14
nm
4
M
- 1
cm
- 1
), the value of R 0 is estimated to be 4.34 nm (Fig. 4C and D). The obtained value of the Förster radius indicates the possibility of the FRET mechanism within the limit of permissibility (1–10 nm). The efficiency of FRET depends on the distance between the donor and the acceptor, which can be calculated using the following relation [45].
(3)
η
%
= 1 -
F
DA
F D

where
F D
is the PL intensity of the donor and
F
DA
is the PL intensity of the donor in the presence of the acceptor. Eq. (3) can be written in terms of corresponding lifetimes as
(4)
η
%
= 1 -
τ
DA
τ D

where
τ D
is the average lifetime of the donor and
τ
DA
is the average lifetime of the donor in the presence of the acceptor. The efficiency of FRET was found to be 30 % at the acceptor concentration of 4
μ
M. Energy transfer efficiency for a donor–acceptor pair at a fixed distance in terms of
R 0
can be expressed as:
(5)
η
FRET
= 1 /
1 +
R /
R 0
6
Where R is the distance between donor and acceptor. Fig. 5 shows the distance-dependent energy transfer efficiency (calculated from eq. (3)) and was fitted with eq. (5).

The rate of energy transfer (
k
ET
) is expressed by the following relation [45].
(6)
k
ET
=
1
τ D
×
R 0
R
6

The quenching of PL of the donor follows the Stern-Volmer (SV) relation as follows [46].
(7)
F D
F
DA
=
1 +
K
SV
Q

Here, [Q] is concentration of the quencher (RB), respectively. The SV coefficient (
K
SV
) was found to be 3.32 × 104 M−1.

We performed the decay-time measurements to investigate the mechanism of energy transfer at the excitation wavelength of 408 nm (Fig. 6 A-D). The PL decay curves were probed at emission wavelengths of donor and acceptor and fitted with the following exponential function.
(8)
I
t
=
∑
i = 1
3
α i
e
- t /
τ i

Where
τ i
are the decay constants along with the corresponding amplitudes
α i
. At 500 nm, the decay curves for NS-GQD show a good fit with the bi-exponential function. It was observed that at lower concentrations (i.e. up to 43 μg/mL) the decay components are 1.37 ns and 4.83 ns. The shorter component corresponds to the radiative decay transition from the intrinsic band, while the longer component is related to the radiative decay transition from the extrinsic band as shown in Fig. 3D. At higher concentrations (100 μg/mL), the decay components increased to 1.83 ns and 5.27 ns, respectively. The increase in the decay components can be explained based on the aggregation-induced self-absorption effects that influence the lifetimes due to
π - π
stacking between the NS-GQD. Fig. 6A displays the PL decay profiles monitored at 500 nm (donor). In presence of the acceptor, the average PL lifetime of the donor decreased from 4.33 ns to 3.73 ns. Fig. 6B shows the PL decay profiles monitored at 600 nm (acceptor). In contrast PL lifetime to the donor, the PL lifetime of acceptor increased from 1.20 ns to 1.73 ns. The decay curve of the acceptor is single exponential. The rise time is too fast to be observed as the solvent is water. The average lifetime of the donor (curve I) in the presence of the acceptor at 500 nm is shown in the Fig. 6E in which curve (II) gives the PL lifetime of acceptor in the donor–acceptor system at 600 nm.

To study the contribution of dynamic quenching of NS-GQD, we used SV relation.
(9)
τ D
τ
DA
=
1 +
K q
τ D
[ Q ]
The linear fit with eq. (9) for both the cases (500 nm and 600 nm) is displayed in the inset of Fig. 6E.

The obtained value of bimolecular quenching constant (
K q
) for the donor is 3 ×
10
3
M
- 1
ns
- 1
and for the case of acceptor, it is −70 ×
10
3
M
- 1
ns
- 1
. The sign of the slope (eq. (9)) indicates the reduction in the PL lifetime of the donor with a concomitant increase in the lifetime of the acceptor. Fig. 6F indicates the decay rates of the FRET pair, respectively. The FRET rate from donor to acceptor is represented by
k
FRET
. The obtained value of
k
FRET
(calculated from eq. (6)) was 0.024
ns
- 1
.

Thus, steady-state and decay time measurements confirms the possibility of resonance energy transfer between the NS-GQD and RB. The functionalization of RB with NS-GQD leads to the development of FRET probe which broadens the scope in terms of sensing because it allow the measurement of emission intensities (both in the steady-state and time-resolved measurements) at two different wavelengths (donor and acceptor) as compared to conventional single wavelength (see supplementary information, section 1).

To compare the usefullness of NS-GQD in cell viability and deep tissue imaging, near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-RCD) were also fabricated by a hydrothermal procedure [47]. Here o-phenylenediamine (oPD) was used as carbon source. The N-RCD shows a prominent PL peak at 632 nm and a shoulder at 675 nm in the excitation range of 420–500 nm (Fig. 7 A).
MCF-7 cells cultured with N-RCD and NS-GQD using MTS assay. Cells cultured on tissue culture plastic (TCP) with complete media was used as control.

N-RCD show excitation-independent PL that can be assigned to the homogeneous microstructure and surface states of the N-RCD [48]. It was also observed that the as prepared N-RCD are non-fluorescent in the range of pH from 7 to 12. The pH sensitivity can be ascribed to protonation and deprotonation of the surface groups and nitrogen in the carbon skeleton [41,47]. Fig. 7B indicates both N-RCD and NS-GQD did not have any toxic effects on the human breast cancer cells and the cells were viable during the culture period of 7 days. However, the proliferation of MCF-7 cells are significantly (p less than 0.05) higher with NS-GQD as compared to those cells cultured with N-RCD. This result indicate that the fabricated N-RCD emits in the NIR region (Fig. 7A) which may be affecting the cell growth as compared to the cells cultured with NS-GQD. As a result, NS-GQD was found to be better suited for experiments of deep tissue imaging applications as compared to N-RCD. It is known that the carbon-based quantum dots developed from bottom-up approach are better biocompatible as these are synthesized in the absence of toxic reactants [49]. In this study, we have performed the synthesis in the water, and this leads to the formation of quantum dots with hydrophilic surface groups. Moreover, GQDs are formed from the carbon elements, which are in general considered to be more biocompatible. The cells were able to proliferate when cultured with both the synthesized materials. The materials did not exert any cytotoxic effects which is attributed to the synthesis process adapted in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-photon absorption process and deep disuse imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the capability of NS-GQD, and NS-GQD-RB nanohybrid systems for nonlinear optical applications, the open aperture (OA) Z-scan profiles were recorded (see supplementary information, section 2). The transmittance of nanohybrid probe (RB and NS-GQD-RB) shows the effect of the saturable absorber (SA). From this, the estimated values of the two-photon absorption (2PA) cross section (
β
) for RB and NS-GQD-RB, respectively are − 5.64 (±0.01) (cm/GW) and −3.75 (±0.01) (cm/GW). For NS-GQD, the SA behaviour is too weak to be measured at 532 nm. It is to be mentioned here that the negative values of
β
indicate the reduction of absorption at higher intensity i.e., behaviour of SA [26,50].

It has been reported that the excitation with visible light results in scattering and reflection which reduces the penetration depth as well as imaging capability [51]. In addition at visible wavelength (532 nm) the nonlinear absorption was found to be weak in NS-GQD. Therefore, the two-photon excitation (2PE) wavelength (780 nm) was selected to increase the penetration depth into the tissue. The schematic diagram for the deep tissue imaging is given in Fig. 8 . Fig. 9 (A-J) gives the 2PE images (z-scan mode) of pigskin tissue after incubation with NS-GQD. 2PA is a nonlinear optical process in which simultaneous absorption of two photons of lower energy can raise the NS-GQD to the same level as attained by the absorption of a single photon of higher energy. The schematic of the energy level diagram for the 2PA is shown in the Fig. 8. It was observed that at the visible wavelength (532 nm) the possibility of 2PA is negligible (see supplementary information). The simultaneous absorption of two photons of lesser energy corresponding to 780 nm excites the NS-GQD via 2PA and the emission of higher energy photon corresponding to 500 nm was observed. This observation suggests that NS-GQD can be used as a fluorescent probe to image the thick sample via 2-photon excitation process.

NS-GQD (100 μg/mL) was incubated into the pigskin tissue. The measurements were performed after 3hr of incubation with near-infrared 2PE wavelength. The NS-GQD based fluorescent nanoprobe shows the capability of recording tissue images up to the penetration depth of 175 μm as shown in Fig. 9 (panel J).

In conclusion, we demonstrated the synthesis of a long wavelength photoluminescence NS-GQD. The results from FTIR and Raman confirm the doping of GQD with the N and S elements and graphitic nature of NS-GQD. AFM and TEM analysis presented in the study suggest the formation of multi-layered NS-GQD of thickness ∼ 2–2.5 nm in the size range of 20–30 nm. The NS-GQD display high PL quantum yield at pH 7 (neutral environment) as compared to acidic or basic environments. NS-GQD show excitation independent PL behavior and the time-resolved emission shows a bi-exponential decay. To further enhance the capabilities of the developed NS-GQD, the NS-GQD were functionalized with RB non-covalently without altering their periodic structure. The nanohybrid probe showed non-radiative resonance energy transfer from NS-GQD to RB. The nanohybrid of NS-GQD-RB composite shows the property of saturable absorption. Cell viability tests indicate that NS-GQD fluorescent nanoprobe enhances the cell proliferation to a greater extent as compared to N-RCD. These NS-GQD were also used to study thick and highly scattering medium, the pigskin, up to the depth of 175 μm under 2PA. The 2PA analysis indicates that the NS-GQD is an active fluorescent probe while N-RCD is inactive.

Vijay Kumar Sagar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Soumyodeep Dey:Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shubhayan Bhattacharya: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Pooria Lesani: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yogambha Ramaswamy: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Gurvinder Singh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Hala Zreiqat: Funding acquisition. Prem Ballabh Bisht: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded under IIT Madras grants (i). TR20210297PHIITM000081 & (ii). PH1920253ALUM000081. This work is also part of the activities of “Centre of Excellence in Biosensing” funded by the Ministry of Education, New Delhi vide project number SB20210819PHMHRD000081. One of us (VKS) thanks University Grant Commission, New Delhi for a fellowship and SD thanks Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi for Inspire fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113618.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
